params.text:
Dignity for people with dementia or communication needs

Dignity is just as important — and sometimes harder to protect — when a person cannot easily tell you what they want. People living with dementia, a learning disability, or a sensory or speech impairment can still sense when they are treated with warmth or dismissed.

  • Assume understanding. Speak to the person, not about them, and never assume they cannot follow you.
  • Give time. Allow longer for someone to process what you say and to respond.
  • Use every channel. A calm tone, eye contact, gentle gestures, pictures or objects can all support communication.
  • Read behaviour as communication. Distress or agitation often signals an unmet need — discomfort, fear or a wish for privacy.
  • Know the person. Life-story information and family involvement help you honour preferences the person may struggle to voice.

params.title: Tap a card to flip it
params.description: How would you protect dignity in each situation? Flip to see good practice.

dialogs.text: A resident with advanced dementia becomes distressed and tearful during personal care.
dialogs.answer: Stop, stay calm and reassure. Distress is communication — she may feel exposed or frightened. Slow down, keep her covered, explain gently and, if needed, try again later.

dialogs.text: A man with aphasia can't find the words to answer your question.
dialogs.answer: Give him time, don't finish his sentences, and offer other ways to respond — pointing, pictures or simple yes/no choices. Never pretend to understand if you don't.

dialogs.text: You don't know how a new resident with dementia likes to be addressed.
dialogs.answer: Check the care plan and life-story information, and ask family. Using someone's preferred name is a simple, powerful mark of respect.
